songs.title: W-W-Web
songs.lyrics:
[Intro — whispered]
w… w… w…
(rounded lips, a low woo)

[Hook 1 — kids chant]
W! W! W-w-w!
What begins with W?
W-w-web! W-w-web!
It's a web! (a web!)

[Hook 2 — kids chant]
W! W! W-w-w!
What begins with W?
W-w-watch! W-w-watch!
It's a watch! (a watch!)

[Hook 3 — kids chant]
W! W! W-w-w!
What begins with W?
W-w-wagon! W-w-wagon!
It's a wagon! (a wagon!)

[Breakdown — whispered, bass drops out]
w… w… w…
w-w-worm… w-w-wolf… w-w-well…

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! Not W!
(w… w… w…)

[Final Hook — big]
W! W! W-w-w!
W-w-wing! W-w-wing!
It's a wing, wing, wing!
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: The W sound is a rounded gliding /w/ — lips pucker then release into a low 'woo', NEVER 'double-you' (letter name) and NEVER 'wuh' (schwa). The '(w… w… w…)' lines are a rounded breathy glide riding under the 808. Answer line 'It's a ___!' reuses the Week 1 frame.

songs.title: The Wet Web
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(drip… drip… drip…)

[Hook]
The web is wet! The web is wet!
What is wet? (wet? wet?)
The web is wet!

[Verse 2]
The wig is wet! The wig is wet!
What is wet? (wet? wet?)
The wig is wet!

[Verse 3]
The bug is wet! The bug is wet!
What is wet? (a bug?!)
The bug is wet!

[Verse 4]
Sam is wet! Sam is wet!
What is wet? (Sam?!)
Sam is wet!

[Outro]
The potato is wet!
(POTATO!)
The web is wet…
(drip… drip… shhh…)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: One frame only: 'The ___ is wet.' — a single word swaps per verse (web → wig → bug → Sam → potato). The rain drips are the percussion bed. Kids choir answers the (wet? wet?) echoes. Everything printed uses the child's real spelling; 'Sam' is a name they read by heart.

spreads.artNote: A single dewy spider web strung across the dark, lit from behind so every strand glows. Empty stage — the children already know this move.
Rain falling on the web, droplets clinging to every thread, glittering. Cold and beautiful.

spreads.text: A wig is on the web!
spreads.artNote: A ridiculous curly wig snagged and dangling from the wet web. First laugh — how did THAT get there?

spreads.text: The bug is on the web.
spreads.artNote: The Bug (red-shelled cast beetle) clinging to the wet web, damp and thoroughly unimpressed. Cast callback.
The Cat (tabby) soaked to the skin, ears flat, glaring at the reader. Peak feline outrage.

spreads.text: The dog is wet and sad.
spreads.artNote: The Dog (brown) drenched and droopy, one paw up, the picture of a sad wet dog.
Sam the peg-doll boy standing under the drip, hair flat, arms out, delighted despite it all.

spreads.text: The web is BIG and wet.
spreads.artNote: Pull back: the web is now enormous, spanning the whole spread, everything stuck to it — wig, bug, a sock, a leaf. Gloriously wet.
The potato, spotlit, impossibly stuck dead-center in the giant wet web. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS the word from the picture. The Bug peeks in from the edge.

sentences: The bug is on the web.
extras: Moveable alphabet: build w-e-t, w-e-b, w-i-g, w-i-n and read them back
Real wet-web science corner: spray a real string web with a mister and watch the drops — say 'the web is wet'
Sound sort: /w/ initial pictures vs the /v/ and /f/ tricksters coming next (wolf vs fan vs van, said aloud)

REUSED FROM WEEK 1 — a potato wearing tiny round glasses standing at a miniature whiteboard with a pointer, teaching, deadpan, ultra-realistic photograph, single subject centered, dramatic spotlight on deep forest-green backdrop, soft shadows, cinematic, slightly whimsical --ar 3:2 --style raw

teacherFocus: Introduce /w/ as a rounded gliding sound (lips pucker — 'woo'), never 'wuh'. Blend w-e-t, w-e-b, w-i-g. The wet-web science corner makes the anchor word real — mist a string web and read 'the web is wet' off the drops. Sneak-preview the /w/–/v/–/f/ lip trio orally; it pays off in Weeks 17 and 22.
